[605]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 绍兴二十年 - The year 1150 in the Southern Song Dynasty, indicating the historical context.
  • 知江州 - Means 'governor of Jiangzhou,' a specific administrative role.
  • 观众客下紫姑神 - Refers to a divination practice involving the 'Purple Girl Spirit,' a deity associated with fortune-telling.
  • 敢问大仙姓名为谁 - A polite way of asking for the name of a deity or spirit.
  • 觊望改秩 - Means 'hope for promotion,' referring to the desire for a higher rank or position.
  • 天机不可泄 - A common phrase meaning 'heavenly secrets cannot be revealed,' often used to deflect direct questions.
  • 书曰 - Means 'the book says,' introducing a citation or revelation.
  • 韦苏州诗 - Refers to a poem by Wei Yaqiao, a poet from Suzhou, indicating a specific literary reference.
  • 洞庭须待满林霜 - A line from the poem, which requires contextual interpretation.
  • 说其母方娠时在衢州 - Means 'his mother was pregnant in Quzhou,' providing a temporal and spatial context.
  • 遣呼乳医 - Means 'sent for a wet nurse,' indicating the care of a newborn.
  • 司法厅 - Refers to the office of the judicial official, a specific administrative location.
  • 涛三岁能言时 - Means 'when Tao was three years old and could speak,' marking a developmental milestone.
  • 常呼人取帽及拄杖 - Means 'he often called for a hat and a cane,' indicating a behavioral pattern.
  • 其家乃知为周君后身也 - Means 'his family then realized he was the reincarnation of Zhou Jun,' indicating a belief in reincarnation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 绍兴二十年 - The year 1150 in the Southern Song Dynasty, a period of significant cultural and political activity.
  • 徐昌言 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official in the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 江州 - The modern-day city of Jiujiang in Jiangxi Province, an important administrative center.
  • 紫姑神 - A deity associated with fortune-telling, often invoked in divination practices.
  • 龚涛仲山 - A historical figure, likely a notable person in the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 周君 - A historical figure, likely a judicial official, whose reincarnation is being described.
  • 洞庭 - Refers to Dongting Lake, a significant geographical feature in southern China, often associated with literary and cultural references.

Proper Nouns

  • 绍兴二十年 - The year 1150 in the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 徐昌言 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official in the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 江州 - The modern-day city of Jiujiang in Jiangxi Province.
  • 紫姑神 - A deity associated with fortune-telling.
  • 龚涛仲山 - A historical figure, likely a notable person in the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 周君 - A historical figure, likely a judicial official.
  • 洞庭 - Refers to Dongting Lake.

[606]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 衢州超化寺 - Chuzhou Chaohua Temple, located in the northern part of the city.
  • 地勢幽閴 - Describes the temple’s location as secluded and quiet.
  • 士大夫多寓居寺后 - Indicates that many scholars and officials lived near the temple.
  • 云山阁 - Refers to the Yunshan Pavilion, a structure near the temple.
  • 阁下寝堂三间 - Describes a three-room sleeping hall under the pavilion.
  • 物怪 - Refers to supernatural or strange phenomena.
  • 举家绕寺求索 - Means the entire family searched the temple for the child.
  • 长老说 - Indicates that the story is told by a senior monk or elder.
  • 嘉陵江边寺 - Refers to a temple by the Jialing River.
  • 奉中大夫王旦 - Refers to Wang Dan, a high-ranking official.
  • 磻石 - Refers to a large, flat stone.
  • 森蔚成行 - Describes the trees as lush and forming rows.
  • 长明灯 - Refers to an eternal lamp, often used in temples.
  • 方丈 - Refers to the main hall of the temple, where the abbot resides.
  • 故人 - Refers to an old friend.
  • 存问交游 - Means to inquire about one’s friends and acquaintances.
  • 西偏小室 - Refers to a small room on the western side.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 衢州 - Refers to Quzhou, a city in Zhejiang Province, China.
  • 超化寺 - A specific temple in Quzhou, historically significant.
  • 士大夫 - Refers to scholars and officials, a key social class in Chinese history.
  • 云山阁 - A cultural landmark, likely a place for scholars and officials to gather.
  • 嘉陵江 - A major river in China, known for its scenic beauty.
  • 磻石 - A term often used in classical Chinese to describe a large, flat stone by a river.
  • 长明灯 - A symbolic element in Chinese temples, representing eternal light and wisdom.
  • 奉中大夫 - A high-ranking official title in the Chinese imperial bureaucracy.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '左右菱芡池数百亩' is elliptical, meaning there are hundreds of acres of lotus and water chestnut ponds on both sides.
  • The phrase '往往见影响' is ambiguous, meaning the family often saw signs or traces of the supernatural.
  • The sentence '唯曾通判独挈家处之' is elliptical, meaning only the Ceng Tongpan (a local official) lived there with his family.
  • The phrase '忆常时所未见也' is elliptical, meaning it was something he had never seen before.
  • The sentence '师去世累岁矣' is elliptical, meaning the monk had passed away many years ago.
  • The phrase '倦欲寐' is elliptical, meaning he was tired and wanted to sleep.

Proper Nouns

  • 衢州 - Quzhou, a city in Zhejiang Province.
  • 超化寺 - Chaohua Temple, a specific temple in Quzhou.
  • 云山阁 - Yunshan Pavilion, a cultural landmark.
  • 嘉陵江 - Jialing River, a major river in China.
  • 磻石 - A large, flat stone by a river.
  • 长明灯 - An eternal lamp, often used in temples.
  • 奉中大夫王旦 - Wang Dan, a high-ranking official.
  • 长老 - A senior monk or elder who narrates the story.

[607]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 戒之曰 - A formal way of saying 'warn him/her,' indicating a solemn instruction.
  • 毋辄出 - 'Do not rashly go out,' where '辄' emphasizes the sudden or hasty nature of the action.
  • 须天且明 - 'Wait until it is almost dawn,' where '且' conveys the idea of 'about to' or 'almost.'
  • 遂去 - 'Then he/she left,' indicating a sequence of events.
  • 旦裴回室中 - 'In the morning, he/she wandered in the room,' where '裴回' suggests aimless or restless movement.
  • 觉境象荒閴 - 'Felt that the surroundings were desolate,' where '荒閴' conveys a sense of emptiness or stillness.
  • 不能睡 - 'Could not sleep,' a straightforward expression of insomnia.
  • 俯窥牕外 - 'Looked down at the window,' where '俯窥' implies a downward glance.
  • 竹影参差 - 'The shadows of bamboo were uneven,' where '参差' describes something irregular or staggered.
  • 心愈动 - 'His/her heart became more restless,' where '愈' emphasizes the increasing intensity.
  • 登床展转 - 'Got into bed and turned restlessly,' where '展转' suggests repeated movement.
  • 目不交睫 - 'His/her eyes did not meet his/her eyelids,' a metaphor for being unable to sleep.
  • 不暇俟其呼 - 'Did not have time to wait for him/her to call,' where '不暇' means 'no time' or 'too busy.'
  • 径起出户 - 'Rushed out of the door,' where '径' suggests a direct or immediate action.
  • 趋就焉 - 'Rushed to it,' where '趋' conveys a sense of urgency.
  • 众方列坐 - 'The crowd was sitting in rows,' where '方' indicates a current state.
  • 数仆以杓行粥 - 'Several servants were ladling out porridge,' where '行粥' means 'ladling porridge.'
  • 钵内炎炎有光 - 'There was a fiery glow inside the bowl,' where '炎炎' describes something burning or glowing.
  • 逼而视之 - 'Pressed closer to look,' where '逼' suggests a forceful or close inspection.
  • 盖镕铜汁也 - 'It was actually molten copper,' where '盖' introduces a clarification or explanation.
  • 热腥逆鼻不可闻 - 'The hot, foul smell was unbearable,' where '逆鼻' means 'offensive to the nose.'
  • 而还 - 'Returned in a panic,' where '' suggests a state of fear or agitation.
  • 复见昨僧 - 'Met the monk from yesterday again,' where '复' indicates repetition.
  • 咄曰 - 'Said sternly,' where '咄' conveys a sharp or commanding tone.
  • 戒君勿出 - 'Warn you not to go out,' where '戒' means 'warn' or 'caution.'
  • 无恐否 - 'Are you not afraid?' where '无恐' means 'not afraid.'
  • 命行者秉炬送归 - 'Ordered a servant to hold a torch and escort him/her back,' where '秉炬' means 'hold a torch.'
  • 中涂炬灭 - 'The torch went out in the middle of the road,' where '中涂' means 'in the middle of the road.'
  • 旦蹶于地 - 'In the morning, he/she stumbled and fell to the ground,' where '蹶' means 'stumble' or 'fall.'
  • 惊而寤 - 'Awakened in a start,' where '寤' means 'awaken.'
  • 则身元在石上 - 'Then realized that he/she was still on the stone,' where '元' means 'originally' or 'still.'
  • 了未尝出 - 'Had not actually left,' where '了' emphasizes the realization.
  • 殆如梦游云 - 'It was almost like a dream,' where '殆' suggests a near or almost state.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 僧堂 - Refers to a Buddhist temple or hall, indicating a place of religious practice.
  • 粥 - Refers to porridge, a common food in Chinese culture.
  • 钵 - Refers to a bowl, often used in religious or monastic contexts.
  • 行者 - Refers to a servant or attendant, often in a religious or monastic setting.
  • 梦游 - Refers to walking in a dream, a common theme in Chinese literature and folklore.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sequence of commands and actions must be read as a series of instructions and their immediate consequences.
  • This long sequence describes the protagonist's internal and external state, leading to a decision to leave.
  • This sequence describes the protagonist's encounter with the monk and the subsequent events, which are both surreal and cautionary.

[608]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 隸兵籍 - Means 'registered in the military records,' indicating past military involvement.
  • 目視荒荒 - Describes eyes as dim or hazy.
  • 白膜蒙其上 - Means 'a white film covers them,' describing the eyes.
  • 冷水调燕支末疗痔疾 - Means 'using cold water to mix rouge powder to treat hemorrhoids.'
  • 狗尾草疗沙石淋 - Means 'using dogtail grass to treat sand and stone urinary tract infections.'
  • 喜饮酒 - Means 'enjoys drinking alcohol.'
  • 醉后略能谈人祸福事 - Means 'after drinking, he can vaguely discuss people's fortunes and misfortunes.'
  • 热疾沉困 - Means 'suffered from a severe heat illness.'
  • 发狂跃入水 - Means 'went mad and jumped into the water.'
  • 渔罔 - Means 'fishing net.'
  • 救出之 - Means 'rescued him.'
  • 汗被体即苏 - Means 'sweat covered his body and he revived.'
  • 临安上省吏孙敏修家 - Refers to the home of an official named Sun Minxiu in Lin'an (modern-day Hangzhou).
  • 卧病 - Means 'lying ill.'
  • 不食七日 - Means 'did not eat for seven days.'
  • 吐利垂死 - Means 'vomiting and diarrhea, nearly dying.'
  • 洪州赵都监书 - Refers to a letter from Zhao, the military supervisor of Hongzhou (modern-day Nanchang).
  • 报赵道人死于洪 - Means 'reporting that Daoist Zhao had died in Hongzhou.'
  • 平时皆与厚善者 - Means 'those who had always been close to him.'
  • 陶婆家 - Refers to the home of a woman named Tao.
  • 道人固无恙 - Means 'the Daoist is still well.'
  • 正尔在孙中奉宅 - Means 'he is actually at Sun's residence.'
  • 二人大骇 - Means 'the two men were greatly startled.'
  • 拜之不己 - Means 'they bowed to him repeatedly.'
  • 默诵真诰中语 - Means 'recited silently from the *Zhen Gao* (True Decree).'
  • 殊不答其说 - Means 'he did not respond to their words.'
  • 往后市街常知班家 - Means 'went to the home of Chang Zhiban on the market street.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 泉州通判李端彦 - Refers to Li Duanyan, a local official in Quanzhou.
  • 绍兴十六年 - Refers to the sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era (1146 CE) during the Southern Song dynasty.
  • 秀州 - Refers to Xiu Prefecture, a region in modern-day Zhejiang Province.
  • 道人赵小哥 - Refers to a Daoist hermit named Zhao Xiaoge.
  • 咸平县 - A specific location, possibly in Henan Province.
  • 真诰 - Refers to the *Zhen Gao* (True Decree), a Daoist scripture.
  • 临安 - Refers to Lin'an, the capital of the Southern Song dynasty (modern-day Hangzhou).
  • 洪州 - Refers to Hongzhou, a region in modern-day Jiangxi Province.

Proper Nouns

  • 泉州 - Quanzhou, a city in Fujian Province.
  • 李端彦 - Li Duanyan, a local official.
  • 绍兴十六年 - The sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era (1146 CE).
  • 秀州 - Xiu Prefecture, a region in modern-day Zhejiang Province.
  • 赵小哥 - Zhao Xiaoge, the protagonist.
  • 咸平县 - A specific location, possibly in Henan Province.
  • 临安 - Lin'an, the capital of the Southern Song dynasty (modern-day Hangzhou).
  • 孙敏修 - Sun Minxiu, an official.
  • 洪州 - Hongzhou, a region in modern-day Jiangxi Province.
  • 赵都监 - Zhao, the military supervisor of Hongzhou.
  • 陶婆 - Tao, a woman.
  • 常知班 - Chang Zhiban, a person.

[609]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 好事者争焚香致敬 - Refers to people with good intentions or those who appreciate the arts competing to burn incense as a sign of respect.
  • 赵拱手凝目 - Describes Zhao bowing his hands and fixing his gaze, indicating a formal and respectful posture.
  • 时举手上下 - Indicates Zhao raising and lowering his hands, possibly as part of a ritual or gesture of communication.
  • 不措一词 - Emphasizes Zhao's silence or restraint.
  • 逮夜 - Indicates a passage of time from day to night.
  • 外人散去 - Suggests that the scene has become private as outsiders leave.
  • 其家遣一子侍 - Indicates that Zhao's family sends a son to serve as an attendant, showing care or attention.
  • 直至晓 - Marks the duration until dawn.
  • 前后门悉开 - Suggests an unusual or deliberate action of opening all front and back doors.
  • 巳不知所在 - Indicates Zhao's sudden disappearance.
  • 久之 - Marks a passage of time after a long period.
  • 复归湖上 - Indicates Zhao's return to the lake.
  • 过李氏坟庵 - Describes Zhao passing by Li's tomb temple.
  • 与端彦相见 - Indicates Zhao meeting with Duan Yan.
  • 尘垢盈体 - Describes Zhao's body being covered in dust and dirt, suggesting a long journey or exposure to the elements.
  • 若远涉万里状 - Emphasizes the distance or effort of Zhao's journey.
  • 问所往 - Indicates an inquiry about Zhao's journey.
  • 不肯言 - Emphasizes Zhao's silence or reluctance to speak.
  • 但云前者为人所戹苦 - Indicates Zhao's past hardships.
  • 且避之 - Suggests Zhao's desire to escape or avoid a situation.
  • 今不敢再入城矣 - Indicates Zhao's change in behavior due to past experiences.
  • 半年又告去 - Indicates Zhao's planned departure six months later.
  • 此地疫起 - Indicates an epidemic breaking out in the area.
  • 吾当治药救人 - Indicates Zhao's intention to make medicine to save people.
  • 去一年然后归 - Indicates Zhao's planned departure and return after a year.
  • 君为道人 - Identifies the person's religious affiliation as a Daoist.
  • 亦畏疫疠乎 - Asks if the person also fears epidemics.
  • 天灾岂可不避 - Indicates a philosophical stance that natural disasters cannot be avoided.
  • 自是还往浸阔 - Indicates a change in relationship where interactions became more distant.
  • 绍兴三十年 - Provides a specific historical context of the thirtieth year of the Shaoxing era (1160 AD).
  • 又来临安 - Indicates Zhao's return to Lin'an (modern-day Hangzhou).
  • 馆于马军王小将家 - Indicates Zhao's accommodation at the home of a small cavalry officer named Wang.
  • 进奏官刘某 - Identifies a certain official named Liu.
  • 以风痹求医 - Indicates Liu seeking medical treatment for rheumatism.
  • 教以薄荷汁搜附子末服之 - Indicates Zhao teaching Liu to mix mint juice with powdered aconite and take it as a medical prescription.
  • 刘饵之过度 - Indicates Liu taking too much of the medicine.
  • 遂死 - Indicates the outcome of Liu's death.
  • 其子归咎欲讼于有司 - Indicates Liu's son blaming Zhao and wanting to sue him in court.
  • 赵曰 - Introduces a direct quote from Zhao.
  • 不须尔 - Indicates Zhao's response to the son's accusation that it is not necessary.
  • 取所余药尽服之 - Indicates Zhao taking all the remaining medicine.
  • 亦死 - Indicates the outcome of Zhao's death.
  • 王氏为买棺 - Indicates the Wang family buying a coffin.
  • 敛而瘗诸小堰门外 - Indicates the burial of the deceased outside the small dam gate.
  • 役者封坎毕 - Indicates the laborers finishing sealing the grave.
  • 还憩门侧粥肆中 - Indicates the return to rest at a nearby soup shop.
  • 见赵在前 - Indicates the visual encounter with Zhao.
  • 呼揖曰 - Indicates calling out and greeting Zhao.
  • 甚苦诸君见送 - Indicates Zhao's farewell, expressing pain at being seen off.
  • 众人异之 - Indicates the others finding Zhao's actions strange.
  • 急返窆处启其柩 - Indicates the quick return to the burial site and opening of the coffin.
  • 空无一物矣 - Indicates the final state of the coffin being empty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 好事者 - Refers to people with good intentions or those who appreciate the arts.
  • 焚香致敬 - A ritual act of burning incense as a sign of respect, commonly practiced in religious and ceremonial contexts.
  • 赵 - The subject of the story, likely a Daoist practitioner or healer.
  • 端彦 - A character who meets Zhao, possibly a friend or acquaintance.
  • 李氏坟庵 - A tomb temple associated with the Li family, indicating a specific location.
  • 绍兴三十年 - The thirtieth year of the Shaoxing era (1160 AD), providing a specific historical context.
  • 临安 - The capital of the Southern Song Dynasty, modern-day Hangzhou, indicating a specific location.
  • 马军王小将 - A small cavalry officer named Wang, indicating a specific person.
  • 进奏官 - An official responsible for reporting to higher authorities, indicating a specific role.
  • 风痹 - Rheumatism, a medical condition.
  • 薄荷汁 - Mint juice, a common ingredient in traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 附子末 - Powdered aconite, a potent and toxic herb used in traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 疫疠 - Epidemics or infectious diseases, indicating a health crisis.
  • 天灾 - Natural disasters, indicating a broader category of calamities.

[610]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 弋猎 - Traditional hunting method using bows and arrows, common in ancient China.
  • 家道粗给 - Indicates a modest but sufficient living standard.
  • 度不可免 - Reflects a sense of inevitability and resignation.
  • 拥土培覆之 - Describes a protective action, showing concern for the young deer.
  • 投于罔中 - A dramatic action that shows self-sacrifice.
  • 破罔出麂 - Demonstrates the mother's bravery and love.
  • 罝罘之属 - Refers to various hunting tools and traps.
  • 焚弃之 - Symbolizes a change in behavior and values.
  • 不复猎 - Shows a moral awakening and a commitment to non-violence.
  • 以计笼取之 - Highlights human ingenuity in dealing with wildlife.
  • 槛数百 - Suggests a large-scale operation to capture monkeys.
  • 微开其板 - Describes a clever trap mechanism.
  • 才可容一猴 - Ensures the trap works effectively.
  • 呼语之曰 - Shows the man's attempt to manipulate the monkeys.
  • 放一枚出 - A cruel method of killing monkeys.
  • 则释汝 - A promise that is never fulfilled.
  • 群猴共执一小者推出之 - Demonstrates the monkeys' cooperation and sacrifice.
  • 民击之以椎 - A brutal act of violence.
  • 即死槛中 - The immediate consequence of the man's actions.
  • 猴望而号呼 - Expresses the monkeys' distress and helplessness.
  • 至于堕泪 - Emphasizes the emotional impact on the monkeys.
  • 索其一 - Continues the cycle of cruelty.
  • 如是至尽乃止 - Describes the systematic and relentless nature of the killings.
  • 土人云 - Provides a local perspective.
  • 麦禾方熟时 - Sets the scene during the harvest season.
  • 猴百十为群 - Describes the social structure of the monkeys.
  • 执臂人立 - Highlights the monkeys' ability to mimic human behavior.
  • 为鱼丽之阵 - Uses a military term to describe the monkeys' formation.
  • 自东而西 - Indicates the direction of their movement.
  • 跳踉数四 - Describes their energetic and destructive actions.
  • 禾尽偃 - Shows the extent of the damage.
  • 攫取之 - Describes the monkeys' method of stealing crops.
  • 余者皆捽踏委去 - Highlights the monkeys' ruthless behavior.
  • 丘中为空 - Describes the aftermath of their actions.
  • 恶而杀之 - Reflects human anger and retaliation.
  • 然亦不仁矣 - Suggests that the act of killing is also unkind.
  • 朱晞颜说 - Attributed to Zhu Xiyan, a historical figure.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 张山人 - Refers to Zhang Shanren, a pseudonym or title for a poet. The term '山人' (shanren) typically denotes a reclusive scholar or hermit.
  • 自山东入京师 - Literally means 'from Shandong entered the capital city,' indicating that Zhang Shanren came from Shandong province to the capital.
  • 以十七字作诗 - Means 'composed poetry with seventeen characters,' referring to his unique style of writing short poems.
  • 著名于元佑绍圣间 - Means 'became famous during the Yuan佑 and Shaoxing periods,' referring to the time when he gained recognition.
  • 至今人能道之 - Means 'until today people can still talk about him,' indicating his lasting fame.
  • 其词虽俚 - Means 'his words are vulgar,' suggesting that his language was simple and colloquial.
  • 然多颖脱 - Means 'but often sharp and insightful,' highlighting the cleverness and wit in his work.
  • 含讥讽 - Means 'contains sarcasm and criticism,' indicating the satirical nature of his poetry.
  • 所至皆畏其口 - Means 'everywhere he went, people feared his tongue,' suggesting that his words were feared due to their sharpness.
  • 争以酒食钱帛遗之 - Means 'competed to give him food, drink, money, and silk,' indicating that people vied to show him hospitality.
  • 年益老 - Means 'as he grew older,' indicating the passage of time.
  • 颇猒倦 - Means 'became somewhat tired and weary,' suggesting a sense of weariness.
  • 乃还乡里 - Means 'then returned to his hometown,' indicating his decision to return home.
  • 未至而死于道 - Means 'before reaching his destination, he died on the road,' indicating the tragic end of his journey.
  • 道旁人亦旧识 - Means 'people by the roadside were also his acquaintances,' suggesting that he was known to those he encountered.
  • 怜其无子 - Means 'pitied him for having no children,' indicating the sympathy for his childlessness.
  • 为买苇席 - Means 'bought reed mats for him,' indicating the practical assistance provided.
  • 束而葬诸原 - Means 'wrapped him and buried him in the fields,' indicating the manner of his burial.
  • 揭木书其上 - Means 'put up a wooden signboard with his name on it,' indicating the marker placed at his grave.
  • 久之 - Means 'after a long time,' indicating the passage of time.
  • 一轻薄子至店侧 - Means 'a frivolous person came to the side of the inn,' indicating the arrival of someone who might be considered superficial.
  • 闻有语及此者 - Means 'heard someone talking about this,' indicating the topic of conversation.
  • 奋然曰 - Means 'boldly said,' indicating the person's reaction.
  • 张翁平生豪于诗 - Means 'Master Zhang was always bold in his poetry,' indicating his reputation for being daring in his poetic style.
  • 今死矣 - Means 'now he is dead,' indicating the current state.
  • 不可无纪述 - Means 'cannot go unrecorded,' indicating the importance of documenting his life.
  • 即命笔题于揭曰 - Means 'immediately ordered a pen and wrote on the signboard,' indicating the act of writing.
  • 此是山人坟 - Means 'This is Master Zhang's tomb,' indicating the inscription.
  • 过者应惆怅 - Means 'passersby should feel sad,' indicating the emotional response expected.
  • 两片芦席包 - Means 'two reed mats wrapped,' indicating the manner of his burial.
  • 饬葬 - Means 'properly buried,' indicating the respectful treatment of his remains.
  • 人以为口业报云 - Means 'people believed it was a retribution for his words,' indicating the interpretation of his death.
  • 吴傅朋说 - Means 'Wu Fupeng said,' indicating the source of the story.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 元佑绍圣间 - Refers to the Yuan佑 (1086-1094) and Shaoxing (1131-1162) periods, which are specific eras in Chinese history.
  • 山东 - Refers to Shandong province, a region in eastern China.
  • 京师 - Refers to the capital city, which in this context is likely referring to the capital during the Yuan佑 and Shaoxing periods.
  • 山人 - A term used to denote a reclusive scholar or hermit, often associated with a certain level of social and cultural status.
  • 轻薄子 - Refers to a frivolous or superficial person, often used to describe someone who lacks depth or seriousness.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 青童神君 - A title of a deity or spiritual figure; likely a specific deity known in Daoist or folk religious traditions.
  • 龍大渊深父 - A name or title, possibly a compound name. '龍' (dragon) is a symbol of power and divinity in Chinese culture. '大渊' (great abyss) may refer to a deep, mysterious place or a symbolic concept. '深父' is less clear; '深' can mean 'deep' or 'profound,' and '父' is 'father.' It may be a title or honorific.
  • 始事潜邸时 - '潜邸' refers to the residence of a prince before ascending the throne; '始事' means 'began to serve' or 'commenced his duties.' This phrase indicates the time when the person was still a prince in the palace.
  • 得伤寒疾 - A classical medical term referring to a severe feverish illness, likely typhoid or influenza-like symptoms. '伤寒' was a term used in classical Chinese medicine.
  • 越五日而汗不出 - '越' here means 'after' or 'passed,' so 'after five days, no sweat came out.' This is a medical description of the progression of the illness.
  • 膝下冷气彻骨 - Describes the coldness in the knees that goes all the way to the bones, a symptom of severe illness.
  • 舌端生白膏 - '白膏' may refer to a white, sticky substance on the tongue, a symptom of illness in classical medical texts.
  • 医者束手 - '束手' is an idiom meaning 'to be at a loss' or 'to have no means to treat,' indicating that the doctors could not help.
  • 以为恶证 - '恶证' means a serious or dangerous illness.
  • 灼艾罢昏寝 - '灼艾' is the act of burning mugwort (a traditional Chinese herbal therapy known as moxibustion). '罢昏寝' means 'after finishing the treatment and going to sleep.'
  • 梦若至诸天阁下 - '诸天' refers to the various heavens or celestial realms in Buddhist or Daoist cosmology. '阁下' is a respectful term, but here it may mean 'below the hall of the heavens' or 'at the foot of the heavenly halls.'
  • 四顾无人 - '四顾' means 'to look all around,' indicating that the dreamer is alone.
  • 仲子乳母 - '仲子' is the second son or a term for a younger son; '乳母' is a wet nurse. This may refer to a specific person in the dream, possibly a caretaker of the prince’s younger brother.
  • 方竚立 - '竚立' is an archaic term for standing or waiting.
  • 有驺导从东来 - '驺导' are attendants or guides; '从东来' means 'coming from the east.' This may refer to a retinue or procession.
  • 身皆长大 - '身' is body or person; '长大' means tall or grown. This could be a description of the attendants.
  • 着淡素宽袍 - '淡素' means plain or simple; '宽袍' is a wide robe. This is a description of their clothing.
  • 巾车垂帘 - '巾车' is a carriage with a veil or canopy; '垂帘' is hanging curtains. This suggests a ceremonial or regal vehicle.
  • 色尽白 - '色' is color; '尽白' means entirely white. This may refer to the attendants or the vehicle being white in color.
  • 杳杳望西北方去 - '杳杳' means distant or obscure; '望西北方去' means 'looking toward the northwest and going away.' This is a directional description in the dream.
  • 行声稍绝 - '行声' is the sound of movement; '稍绝' means it gradually fades away.
  • 又有继其后者 - '继其后者' means 'there were more following behind.'
  • 侍卫皆青衣女童 - '侍卫' is attendants or guards; '青衣' is blue or green clothing; '女童' is young girls. This is a description of the attendants.
  • 各执芙蓉花 - '执' is to hold; '芙蓉花' is the lotus flower, a symbol of purity and divinity in Chinese culture.
  • 麾纛旗幢 - '麾' is to wave or command; '纛' is a military standard; '旗幢' are banners. This may refer to the attendants waving banners or flags.
  • 夹列左右 - '夹列' is to line up on both sides; '左右' is left and right. This describes the attendants arranged on either side.
  • 一人乘辂如王者 - '辂' is a ceremonial carriage; '如王者' is like a king or ruler. This is a description of the central figure.
  • 戴卷云玉冠 - '卷云' is swirling clouds; '玉冠' is a jade crown. This is a description of the figure's headgear.
  • 被青衣 - '被' is to wear; '青衣' is green or blue clothing.
  • 两绶自顶垂至腰 - '绶' is a sash or ribbon; '自顶垂至腰' means from the top of the head down to the waist. This is a description of the sash.
  • 缥缥然 - '缥缥' is a term for a light, ethereal appearance, possibly indicating a divine or otherworldly presence.
  • 容貌清整 - '容貌' is appearance; '清整' is clear and orderly, suggesting a refined or noble look.
  • 微有须 - '微' is slight; '须' is beard. This suggests the figure has a slight beard, indicating youth or maturity.
  • 似十三四岁男子 - '似' is like; '十三四岁' is about 13 or 14 years old. This describes the figure's age.
  • 辂既过 - '辂' is the carriage; '既过' means 'after it had passed.'
  • 一女童招深父使前 - '招' is to beckon; '使前' is to make go forward. This is an action by the girl.
  • 顾曰 - '顾' is to look back; '曰' is to say. This is a dialogue marker.
  • 识车中尊神乎 - '识' is to recognize; '尊神' is a revered deity. This is a question asking if the person recognizes the deity in the carriage.
  • 曾施敬否 - '曾' is have; '施敬' is offered reverence; '否' is no. This is a question about whether reverence was offered.
  • 曰。甚善甚善 - '曰' is said; '甚善甚善' is 'very good, very good,' an expression of approval.
  • 此青童神君也 - '此' is this; '青童神君' is the deity’s name.
  • 使子遇白舆中人 - '使' is if; '子' is you; '遇' is to meet; '白舆中人' is a person in a white carriage. This is a conditional statement.
  • 已成齑粉 - '齑粉' is powder or dust, used metaphorically to mean destroyed or annihilated.
  • 然当再回 - '然' is however; '当再回' is 'will return again.'
  • 不可不避 - '不可' is not possible; '不避' is not to avoid. This is a warning to avoid the danger.
  • 以手中花予深父 - '以' is with; '手中花' is the flower in the hand; '予' is to give; '深父' is the person. This is the act of giving a flower.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 暴死 - Literally 'sudden death,' referring to a sudden and unexpected death.
  • 四日而蘇 - 'Four days later, he revived' or 'regained consciousness.' Common phrase in classical Chinese to describe a near-death experience followed by recovery.
  • 追至冥府 - 'Was taken to the Underworld.' The phrase '追至' suggests being led or followed to a place, here the afterlife.
  • 主者据案 - 'The official sat at the desk.' '主者' is a general term for the official in charge, often used in descriptions of the afterlife.
  • 皂吏满前 - 'Black-robed officials were all around.' '皂吏' refers to officials in black robes, a common visual marker of bureaucratic or underworld officials in classical texts.
  • 引问平生既毕 - 'After questioning him about his entire life.' This is a formal phrase indicating a complete review of a person’s life.
  • 授以铁棰 - 'Was given an iron staff.' This is a symbolic act of assigning a role in the afterlife.
  • 使为狱卒 - 'To be a jailer.' A common role assigned to the dead in underworld narratives.
  • 立殿下 - 'Standing beneath the hall.' This likely refers to a position of service in the underworld court.
  • 凡呼他囚姓名 - 'Whenever he called out the names of other prisoners.' Indicates the role of a jailer in summoning prisoners.
  • 即与同列驱而进 - 'He would be driven forward with his colleagues.' This implies a ritualistic or hierarchical process of punishment.
  • 吏前数其过恶 - 'The official would list his offenses.' '数其过恶' is a formal way of enumerating one’s sins or misdeeds.
  • 令持棰笞击 - 'Ordered him to strike with the staff.' This is a punishment meted out by the jailer.
  • 应手为血 - 'At the touch of the staff, blood flowed.' A vivid image of the punishment’s effect.
  • 以水噀之 - 'With water, he was sprinkled.' This is a purification ritual, often used to symbolize the restoration of life or innocence.
  • 乃复为人 - 'He would then become a person again.' Indicates the temporary return to a human state.
  • 如是者非一 - 'This was not the only instance.' Implies that this process was repeated multiple times.
  • 良久事稍间 - 'After a long time, the affairs became less busy.' Indicates a period of inactivity or lull.
  • 纵步庑下 - 'He walked freely under the eaves.' A phrase indicating leisure or freedom of movement.
  • 榜曰判官院 - 'A sign read 'Office of the Judge.' '榜' refers to a sign or plaque.
  • 陈列帏帐几格 - 'Displayed with curtains, tables, and shelves.' Describes the interior of a room or office.
  • 细视其人 - 'Looking closely at the person.' Indicates careful observation.
  • 盖故主翁王蕴监税也 - 'It was the former master, Mr. Wang Yun, the tax official.' '盖' is a particle used to introduce a clarification or explanation.
  • 询所以来 - 'He asked why he was here.' '询' is to inquire, '所以来' is the reason for coming.
  • 备言始末 - 'He fully explained the beginning and end.' A formal phrase for giving a complete account.
  • 且力丐归 - 'And he earnestly begged to return.' '丐' is to beg or implore.
  • 蕴许诺 - 'Wang Yun agreed.' A simple confirmation of permission.
  • 与俱过他府 - 'He went with him to another office.' Indicates moving to a different location.
  • 令坐门外 - 'Ordered him to sit outside.' A formal instruction to wait.
  • 须臾出呼曰 - 'A short while later, he came out and called.' '须臾' is a classical term for a short time.
  • 汝未当来此 - 'You were not meant to come here.' Indicates a mistake in the assignment or a correction.
  • 今可复生 - 'Now you may return to life.' A direct command or statement of release.
  • 手书牒见付 - 'He gave him a written document by hand.' '牒' is a formal document or warrant.
  • 使亟还 - 'Ordered him to return quickly.' '亟' means urgently or quickly.
  • 且云 - 'And he said.' A transitional phrase.
  • 我在此极不恶 - 'I am here and it is not bad at all.' Indicates contentment with the current state.
  • 但乏钱及纸笔为用 - 'But I lack money and paper and writing utensils to use.' A humble request for resources.
  • 汝归语吾家 - 'When you return, tell my family.' A final instruction before the return.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 冥府 - The Underworld, a common concept in Chinese cosmology and religious belief, often associated with the afterlife and judgment.
  • 主者 - A term used for the chief official in the underworld, often equivalent to a judge or ruler of the dead.
  • 皂吏 - Officials in black robes, a standard visual marker of bureaucratic or underworld officials in classical Chinese texts.
  • 铁棰 - An iron staff or rod, often used as a symbol of authority or punishment.
  • 狱卒 - A jailer or prison guard, a role often assigned to the dead in underworld narratives.
  • 殿下 - A term of respect for the ruler or superior, here likely referring to the throne or hall of the underworld ruler.
  • 判官院 - The office or chamber of the judge, a common feature in Chinese underworld imagery.
  • 王蕴监税 - A historical or fictional figure, likely a tax official. The name '王蕴' (Wang Yun) is a common Chinese surname and given name, though it is not a well-known historical figure.
  • 牒 - A formal document or warrant, often used in legal or bureaucratic contexts.
  • 冥卒 - A term for a soul-escort or official in the afterlife, which is the title of the story itself.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The pair of sentences '原皆起于人心。人心存而有之。' must be read as a single unit, with the first sentence introducing the idea that everything originates from the human heart/mind, and the second sentence reinforcing that the human heart/mind inherently contains these phenomena.
  • The pair of sentences '无无有有。盖无所致诘。' must be read as a single unit, with the first sentence stating a philosophical principle, and the second sentence indicating that there is no need to question further.
  • The pair of sentences '又语沄问学。曰、当读睿智显谟两先生文集。' must be read as a single unit, with the first sentence introducing a continuation of the discussion on learning, and the second sentence providing specific advice on what to read.
  • The pair of sentences '告以世无此书。曰、书已为秦政焚灭矣。' must be read as a single unit, with the first sentence stating that there is no such book in the world, and the second sentence explaining why.
  • The pair of sentences '承烈先生者、显谟先生子也。其意盖指帝尧及文王武王。' must be read as a single unit, with the first sentence introducing a specific person, and the second sentence explaining the significance of that person.
  • The triplet of sentences '又曰。人无信不立。果知自信。则先王之道、可由学而致。' must be read as a single unit, with the first sentence emphasizing the importance of trust, the second sentence reinforcing the importance of self-confidence, and the third sentence linking self-confidence to understanding the ways of the ancients.
  • The pair of sentences '宣外甥久病疟。女兄睹此事敬异之。' must be read as a single unit, with the first sentence introducing a medical condition, and the second sentence describing a reaction to that condition.
  • The pair of sentences '神即傍顾曰。闻亲戚间有鬼疟。可并案也。' must be read as a single unit, with the first sentence describing a supernatural event, and the second sentence explaining the nature of that event.
  • The pair of sentences '安之不许。明日、女兄来假室治甥病。' must be read as a single unit, with the first sentence describing a refusal, and the second sentence describing a subsequent action.
  • The triplet of sentences '神降者三人。其一类左司公。呼宣小字曰、虎儿。吾汝父也。' must be read as a single unit, with the first sentence describing a supernatural event, the second sentence identifying a specific type of spirit, and the third sentence providing a personal address.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '未第时，为成都某氏馆客' is elliptical, omitting the subject ('杨希仲') in the second part. It should be understood as 'When he was not yet successful in the exams, he stayed as a guest scholar at a family in Chengdu.'
  • The phrase '欲与绸缪' is ambiguous, as it could refer to either intimate relations or a more general sense of closeness. The context suggests the former, but it is important to note the potential ambiguity.
  • The verb '遂去' is elliptical, meaning 'she left' or 'she departed,' but it could also imply that she was rebuked or felt ashamed.
  • The phrase '岁暮夫归' is elliptical, meaning 'at the end of the year, when he returned home,' but it could also imply that the husband returned after a long absence.
  • The phrase '冀有所遇' is elliptical, meaning 'hoping to encounter something,' but it could also imply that the person was seeking a spiritual or supernatural experience.
  • The phrase '顽块也' is elliptical, meaning 'it is just a clay block,' but it could also imply that the object has deeper significance beyond its physical form.
  • The phrase '始疑为异人' is elliptical, meaning 'he began to suspect it was a supernatural being,' but it could also imply that the person was unsure of the object's true nature.

  • 命席對酌 - Ordered a seat and began to drink, suggesting a formal or ceremonial setting for drinking.
  • 視當垆婦絕似前夕所偶者 - Looked at the woman tending the wine shop and found her strikingly similar to the one encountered the previous night; '絕似' emphasizes the striking resemblance.
  • 唯顏色萎黃為不類 - Only her complexion, which was pale and yellow, was different; highlights a contrast in appearance.
  • 婦亦頻屬目 - The woman also frequently glanced at him, indicating her attention or suspicion.
  • 類有所疑 - Seemed to have some doubt, suggesting uncertainty or suspicion.
  • 呼問之 - Called out to ask her, indicating a direct interaction.
  • 對曰 - She replied, marking the beginning of her response.
  • 兩日前夢人召至一处 - Two days ago, I dreamed that someone summoned me to a place, introducing a dream narrative.
  • 少年郎留连竟夕 - A young man kept me company all night, describing the interaction in the dream.
  • 暨睡醒 - Until I woke up, marking the end of the dream.
  • 体中殊不佳 - My body felt very unwell, indicating a physical reaction.
  • 血下如注几二斗乃止 - Blood flowed like a stream, nearly two dou (a unit of measure) before stopping, describing a severe physical condition.
  • 今猶奄奄短气 - Now I still feel weak and short of breath, indicating lingering effects.
  • 平生未尝感此疾也 - I have never felt this illness before, emphasizing the unusual nature of the condition.
  • 始悟所致盖其魂云 - I began to realize that it was caused by her soul, indicating a supernatural interpretation.
  • 虞丞相自荆襄召还 - Grand Councillor Yu was summoned back from Jing and Xiang, referring to a historical figure and his return.
  • 子公亮遣书扣所向 - Zi Gongliang sent a letter inquiring about his future, indicating a request for information.
  • 杨答曰 - Yang replied, marking the beginning of Yang's response.
  • 得苏不得苏 - If I get Su, I won't get Su, a riddle or paradoxical statement.
  • 半月去作同签书 - After half a month, I will become a deputy signatory, indicating a future appointment.
  • 虞公以谓签书不带同字已久 - Grand Councillor Yu thought that the title of deputy signatory without the 'tong' prefix had been used for a long time, indicating a historical context.
  • 既而守苏台 - After that, I will govern Suzhou, indicating a future role.
  • 到官十五日 - After fifteen days in office, indicating a short period.
  • 召为同签书枢密院事 - I was summoned to be a deputy signatory of the Military Council, indicating a significant appointment.
  • 时钱处和先为签书 - At that time, Qian Chuhe was already a deputy signatory, providing historical context.
  • 故加同字 - Therefore, the 'tong' prefix was added, explaining the title change.
  • 如此类甚多 - There are many such cases, indicating a broader context.
  • 不胜载 - Too numerous to recount, emphasizing the frequency of such events.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 以有升甲恩，特旨列于五甲末 - Due to the grace of promotion to the top rank, he was specially listed at the bottom of the top five ranks.
  • 倒置之意 - The intention of being placed upside down or the purpose of being inverted.
  • 士子十数辈将应举，来谒唐 - A dozen or so scholars, who were about to take the imperial examinations, came to visit Tang.
  • 员显道与宗家以肉菹作饼，食而余其四 - Yuan Xian Dao and his family made a cake with pickled meat, ate it, and left four pieces.
  • 适无一物，可以为先生供 - Just now there was nothing, but it can serve as your offering.
  • 肉饼尚有四枚，何靳也 - There are still four pieces of meat cake, why be stingy?
  • 凡所见皆类此 - All that was seen was similar to this.
  • 成都村民挽车入市，逢道人，遗交子二千 - A villager from Chengdu pulled a cart into the market, met a Taoist, and left behind two thousand jiaozi.
  • 倩汝送与仙井唐八郎 - Ask you to deliver it to Tang Ba Lang in Xianjing.
  • 同辈稍黠者咤曰，吾闻八郎异人也 - A slightly cleverer companion exclaimed, 'I heard that Ba Lang is a strange person.'
  • 书中得非有奇药方书乎 - Is there not a strange medicine or secret book in the letter?
  • 发视之，白纸也 - Opened it to see, and it was just white paper.
  • 急复缄封之，才至仙井 - Quickly sealed it again, and just arrived at Xianjing.
  • 迎骂曰，何不还吾书 - Met and scolded, saying, 'why didn't you return my letter?'
  • 再拜谢罪，执书再三读 - Prostrated twice and apologized, held the letter and read it repeatedly.
  • 叹曰，又迟了我二十四年 - Said with a sigh, 'it delayed me by twenty-four years.'
  • 不乐而去，至今犹存 - Left in a bad mood, and still exists to this day.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夷坚丙志 - Title of the collection, *Yijian* (Strange Tales), specifically the *Bing* volume.
  • 青城道会 - Refers to a Daoist assembly held in Qingcheng, a famous Daoist mountain in Sichuan Province.
  • 会者万计 - Literally means 'thousands of people gathered,' emphasizing the large number of participants.
  • 县民往往旋结屋山下 - Describes how local residents often built temporary shelters near the mountain.
  • 以鬻茶果 - Means 'to sell tea and fruits,' indicating the commercial activity of the locals.
  • 饼店道人 - Refers to a Daoist who operates a bakery.
  • 初启肆之日 - Means 'on the first day of opening the shop.'
  • 一客被酒造其居 - Describes a drunken guest who arrives at the bakery.
  • 醉语无度 - Means 'speaks drunkenly without restraint.'
  • 袒卧门左 - Describes the guest lying naked on the left side of the door.
  • 饼师殊苦之 - Means 'the baker is greatly troubled by him.'
  • 与之钱不受 - Means 'giving him money, he refuses.'
  • 饲以饼不纳 - Means 'offering him a cake, he does not accept.'
  • 先是有风折大木 - Describes a previous event where a strong wind broke a large tree.
  • 居民析为二橙 - Means 'the residents split it into two benches.'
  • 正临门侧 - Means 'right next to the door.'
  • 以待过者 - Means 'to wait for passersby.'
  • 店去江颇远 - Means 'the shop is far from the river.'
  • 方汲水二器 - Means 'just when he was fetching two containers of water.'
  • 未及用 - Means 'before he could use them.'
  • 客忽起 - Means 'the guest suddenly stands up.'
  • 缚茆帚蘸水 - Means 'ties a bundle of straw and dips it in water.'
  • 洗木搰搰 - Means 'washes the wood with a scraping sound.'
  • 逾两时 - Means 'for more than two hours.'
  • 又卧其上 - Means 'then lies on top of it again.'
  • 往来望见者皆恶之 - Means 'those who see him pass by all hate him.'
  • 及门即返 - Means 'as soon as they reach the door, they turn back.'
  • 饼终日不得泄 - Means 'the bakery cannot sell all day long.'
  • 客亦舍去 - Means 'the guest also leaves.'
  • 谢主人曰、毋怒我 - Means 'apologizes to the owner, saying, 'Do not be angry with me.''
  • 我明日携钱偿汝直 - Means 'I will bring money tomorrow to pay you.'
  • 当倍售矣 - Means 'the sales will double.'
  • 遂行 - Means 'and leaves.'
  • 或诣橙旁欲坐 - Means 'some people go to the bench and want to sit.'
  • 见光采烂然 - Means 'see a bright and dazzling light.'
  • 乃浓墨大书吕先生来四字 - Means 'they see the four characters 'Mr. Lü has come' written in thick ink.'
  • 取刀削之 - Means 'take a knife to scrape it off.'
  • 愈削愈明 - Means 'the more they scrape, the clearer it becomes.'
  • 深透木底 - Means 'penetrating deep into the wood.'
  • 上下若一 - Means 'as if it were one piece.'
  • 观者如堵 - Means 'the spectators are like a wall.'
  • 自此饼果大售 - Means 'from then on, the cakes and fruits sell very well.'
  • 时绍兴三十二年二月 - Specifies the time as the second month of the thirty-second year of the Shaoxing era.
  • 关寿卿亲见其洗木时 - Means 'Guan Shouqing personally saw him washing the wood.'
  • 云一清瘦道人也 - Means 'he said it was a thin and slender Daoist.'

  • 绍兴丁巳岁 - Shaoxing is the reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Song; 丁巳 is a traditional Chinese stem-branch calendar year, corresponding to 1147 CE.
  • 尚书 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the Song government, possibly the Shangshu Ling (Minister of Works), or the title of a Shangshu (a high-ranking minister).
  • 赵使君 - 使君 is a title for a high-ranking local official, often a military governor or magistrate; 赵 is likely the surname of the official.

  • 舉家驚欲痴 - Idiomatic phrase meaning 'the whole family was shocked and nearly stunned.' Combines 驚 (shock) and 欲痴 (as if to become dazed), suggesting an overwhelming emotional reaction.
  • 與一馬同毙 - 'Died together with a horse.' The verb 毙 (to die) is used in a metaphorical or literal sense to describe a simultaneous death.
  • 英忠未立祠 - 'The loyal and valiant did not yet have a shrine.' 英忠 is a compound term meaning 'brave and loyal,' often used to describe heroes or warriors.
  • 滿考遷京畿 - 'After completing his term of office, he was transferred to the capital.' 滿考 refers to the completion of an official term, and 遷京畿 refers to a promotion or reassignment to the capital region.
  • 兄弟列三戟 - 'His brothers held three halberds (symbols of office).' 三戟 is a metaphor for high-ranking official status, as 戟 (halberd) was a ceremonial weapon of officials.
  • 神則加冕衣 - 'The god was then given a crown and ceremonial robe.' 加冕衣 is a metaphor for divine investiture or elevation.
  • 寒温五种疟 - 'Five kinds of malaria related to cold and heat.' 寒温 refers to temperature extremes, and 疟 is a classical term for malaria.

  • 或能为病祟 - 'They may cause illness or supernatural harm.' 病祟 refers to illness caused by supernatural or demonic forces.

  • 来者犹可追 - 'The future can still be pursued.' A common expression meaning that it is not too late to change course.
  • 经营数楹屋 - 'To build several rooms or houses.' 经营 means to plan or construct, and 数楹屋 refers to a small house or structure.
  • 丰俭随公私 - 'The wealth or poverty depends on the public or private.' A balanced expression about how resources are allocated based on need or status.

  • 鼋死 - 死 means to die. Describes the turtle's death.
  • 始桌舟牺岸 - 始 means then, 桌舟 means to row the boat, and 牺岸 means to dock at the shore. Describes the fisherman rowing the boat to the shore.
  • 邦人观者如堵 - 邦人 means people of the country, 观者 means spectators, and 如堵 means as thick as a wall. Describes the crowd of spectators.
  • 喜其去害 - 喜 means to be happy, 其 means that, and 去害 means to remove the harm. Describes the people's happiness at the removal of the threat.
  • 争出钱与之 - 争 means to compete, 出钱 means to give money, and 与之 means to him. Describes the people competing to give money to the fisherman.
  • 盖鼋性嗜猪肝 - 盖 means because, 鼋性 means the nature of the turtle, and 嗜猪肝 means loves pig liver. Explains why the turtle was attracted to the liver.
  • 渔者知之 - 知之 means knew it. Describes the fisherman's knowledge of the turtle's nature.
  • 又得操纵之术 - 又得 means also obtained, 操纵 means to manipulate, and 之术 means the method. Describes the fisherman's skill in manipulating the situation.
  • 故为力甚易 - 故 means therefore, 为力 means to exert force, and 甚易 means very easy. Describes how easy the task was for the fisherman.
  • 仲秉说 - 仲秉 is the name of the author or narrator, and 说 means to say or to narrate. Introduces the narrator.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 叶急持其袂 - 叶急 (Yè jí) is an unusual name, likely a person's name, and 持其袂 (chí qí mèi) means 'to hold their sleeve.' The phrase describes a physical action.
  • 盗虑不得脱 - 盗虑 (dào lǜ) means 'the thief considers,' and 不得脱 (bù dé tuō) means 'cannot escape.' The phrase conveys the thief's thought process.
  • 掣其肘曰 - 掣其肘 (chè qí zhǒu) is an idiom meaning 'to restrain someone's arm,' and 曰 (yuē) means 'says.' The phrase describes the thief's action and speech.
  • 放我。不然。将杀汝 - 放我 (fàng wǒ) means 'let me go,' 不然 (bù rán) means 'otherwise,' and 将杀汝 (jiāng shā rǔ) means 'I will kill you.' The phrase is a direct threat.
  • 叶醉甚。持之愈急 - 叶醉甚 (yè zuì shèn) means '叶 is very drunk,' and 持之愈急 (chí zhī yù jí) means 'holds him more tightly.' The phrase describes the increased intensity of the action.
  • 盗恐众至。乃剚刃而去 - 盗恐众至 (dào kǒng zhòng zhì) means 'the thief fears that others will arrive,' and 乃剚刃而去 (nǎi zì rèn ér qù) means 'and then thrusts the blade and leaves.' The phrase describes the thief's action and departure.
  • 叶即死 - 叶即死 (yè jí sǐ) means '叶 dies immediately.' The phrase describes the outcome.
  • 二尉闻之。惧以是坐罪 - 二尉闻之 (èr wèi wén zhī) means 'the two magistrates heard of it,' and 惧以是坐罪 (jù yǐ shì zuò zuì) means 'fear that they will be held responsible for this.' The phrase describes the magistrates' reaction.
  • 迹捕未获 - 迹捕 (jì bǔ) means 'to trace and capture,' and 未获 (wèi huò) means 'not caught.' The phrase describes the unsuccessful pursuit.
  • 见叶从庑下掩腹入僧房 - 见叶从庑下掩腹入僧房 (jiàn yè cóng wǔ xià yǎn fù rù sēng fáng) means 'saw叶from under the eaves, covering his abdomen, enter the monk's room.' The phrase describes a mysterious event.
  • 左右无一睹者 - 左右无一睹者 (zuǒ yòu wú yī dǔ zhě) means 'no one on the left or right saw it.' The phrase emphasizes the unusual nature of the event.
  • 邑有女巫。能通鬼神事 - 邑有女巫 (yì yǒu nǚ wū) means 'there is a village witch,' and 能通鬼神事 (néng tōng guǐ shén shì) means 'who can communicate with spirits.' The phrase introduces a supernatural element.
  • 遣询之 - 遣询之 (qiǎn xún zhī) means 'sent to ask her.' The phrase describes the action of seeking information.
  • 方及门 - 方及门 (fāng jí mén) means 'just as she reached the door.' The phrase describes the timing of the event.
  • 巫举止言语如叶平生 - 巫举止言语如叶平生 (wū jǔ zhǐ yán yǔ rú yè píng shēng) means 'the witch's manner and speech were like叶's when he was alive.' The phrase describes the witch's behavior.
  • 大恸曰 - 大恸曰 (dà tòng yuē) means 'cried bitterly and said.' The phrase describes the witch's emotional response and speech.
  • 为我谢二尉 - 为我谢二尉 (wèi wǒ xiè èr wèi) means 'on my behalf, thank the two magistrates.' The phrase describes the witch's request.
  • 以宿业不幸死 - 以宿业不幸死 (yǐ sù yè bù xìng sǐ) means 'because of my past karma, I died unfortunately.' The phrase explains the cause of death.
  • 今已得凶人 - 今已得凶人 (jīn yǐ dé xiōng rén) means 'now I have caught the villain.' The phrase describes the witch's current state.
  • 更数日就擒 - 更数日就擒 (gèng shù rì jiù qín) means 'in a few more days, I will be captured.' The phrase describes the future event.
  • 无所憾 - 无所憾 (wú suǒ hàn) means 'have no regrets.' The phrase describes the witch's emotional state.
  • 独念母老且贫 - 独念母老且贫 (dú niàn mǔ lǎo qiě pín) means 'only think of my old and poor mother.' The phrase describes the witch's concern.
  • 吾囊中所贮。可及百千 - 吾囊中所贮。可及百千 (wú náng zhōng suǒ zhù. kě jí bǎi qiān) means 'the money I have in my bag, about a hundred thousand.' The phrase describes the amount of money.
  • 望为火吾骸 - 望为火吾骸 (wàng wéi huǒ wú hái) means 'hope to burn my bones.' The phrase describes the witch's request.
  • 收遗骨及余赀与母 - 收遗骨及余赀与母 (shōu yí gǔ jí yú zī yǔ mǔ) means 'collect my remains and the remaining money for my mother.' The phrase describes the witch's request.
  • 则存没受赐矣 - 则存没受赐矣 (zé cún mò shòu cì yǐ) means 'then, both alive and dead, I will be grateful.' The phrase describes the witch's gratitude.
  • 尉悉如所戒 - 尉悉如所戒 (wèi xī rú suǒ jiè) means 'the magistrates followed all the instructions.' The phrase describes the magistrates' actions.
  • 后五日。果得盗 - 后五日。果得盗 (hòu wǔ rì. guǒ dé dào) means 'five days later, the thief was indeed caught.' The phrase describes the outcome.
  • 盗言杀业之次日 - 盗言杀业之次日 (dào yán shā yè zhī cì rì) means 'the thief said the next day after killing叶.' The phrase describes the thief's statement.
  • 即见诸百步外 - 即见诸百步外 (jí jiàn zhū bǎi bù wài) means 'saw叶hundreds of steps away.' The phrase describes the thief's perception.
  • 已而渐近 - 已而渐近 (yǐ ér jiàn jìn) means 'then gradually got closer.' The phrase describes the changing distance.
  • 昨乃与同卧起 - 昨乃与同卧起 (zuó nǎi yǔ tóng wò qǐ) means 'yesterday, I slept and woke up with him.' The phrase describes the thief's experience.
  • 自知必败云 - 自知必败云 (zì zhī bì bài yún) means 'knowing that he would surely be defeated.' The phrase describes the thief's realization.

  • 授以针指、敏捷工致 - Taught her needlework, and she was quick and skillful; *针指* refers to sewing or needlework; *敏捷工致* means quick and skilled.
  • 一家怜爱焉 - The whole family loved and pitied her; indicates that she was accepted and cared for.
  • 士亦稍与之昵 - The man also gradually grew close to her; *昵* means to be close or intimate.
  • 居一年许 - After living for about a year; *许* is a particle indicating approximation.
  • 出游相国寺 - To go out and visit Xiangguo Temple; *相国寺* is the name of a Buddhist temple in the capital city, likely in Luoyang or Chang’an.
  • 骇曰 - Shocked and said; indicates the reaction of the monk.
  • 子妖气甚盛 - You have a very strong demonic or supernatural aura; *妖气* refers to an aura associated with the supernatural or evil.
  • 奈何 - What will you do? A rhetorical question expressing concern.
  • 以为诳已 - Thought it was a lie; *诳* means to deceive or lie.
  • 怒不应 - Angered and did not respond; indicates the man’s reaction to the monk’s warning.
  • 异日再见曰 - On another day, when he saw him again, he said; indicates a later encounter.
  • 祟急矣 - The supernatural affliction is urgent; *祟* refers to a supernatural disturbance or haunting.
  • 子其实语我 - You must truly tell me; a plea for honesty.
  • 我无求于子也 - I have no request of you; indicates the monk is not seeking anything in return.
  • 家岂有古器若折足铛鼎之属乎 - Does your family have any ancient objects like broken-footed cauldrons or tripods? *折足铛鼎* refers to ancient bronze vessels with broken legs, often used in religious or ancestral rituals.
  • 曰。无之 - He said, 'No'; indicates the man’s denial.
  • 问不已 - He kept asking; indicates persistence.
  • 士不能掩 - The man could not hide it; indicates he was forced to reveal the truth.
  • 始以妾告 - He finally told him she was his妾; *妾* refers to a concubine or secondary wife.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 夷堅丙志 - Yijian (A Collection of Strange Tales) is a classical Chinese literary work attributed to the Southern Song dynasty author Zhang Zhihe. It is a collection of strange stories and supernatural events, often involving ghosts, spirits, and unusual human conditions. *夷堅丙志* is the third of several volumes in this series.
  • 無足婦人 - The title of the story, which describes a woman without legs or feet. In classical Chinese literature, such physical anomalies often have supernatural or symbolic significance.
  • 朝士 - A term used for a man holding an official position in the imperial court. This suggests the man who encounters the woman is of the educated and bureaucratic class.
  • 相国寺 - Xiangguo Temple was a famous Buddhist temple in the capital city of the Song dynasty, likely in Luoyang or Kaifeng. It was a center of religious and cultural activity.
  • 折足铛鼎 - Refers to ancient bronze vessels used in ritual sacrifices or ceremonies. These were often considered sacred objects and were used in ancestral worship. The mention of such objects may indicate a supernatural or ancestral connection.
  • 妾 - A *妾* is a concubine or secondary wife in a traditional Chinese household. This term reflects the social hierarchy and the status of women in classical Chinese society.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 士 - A scholar or gentleman, typically a member of the educated elite in classical Chinese society.
  • 相国寺 - Xiangguo Temple, a famous Buddhist temple in Luoyang, historically significant in Chinese religious and cultural history.
  • 道人 - A term for a Daoist or Buddhist monk, depending on context. Here, it likely refers to a Buddhist monk given the temple reference.
  • 本命神 - 'Guardian deity' or 'natal deity,' a concept in Chinese cosmology and folk religion, where each person is believed to have a deity associated with their birth date or life force.
  • 逆旅 - An inn or lodging house, a common term in classical Chinese literature for temporary accommodation.
  • 黑马 - A black horse, often symbolizing mystery, strength, or supernatural power in classical texts.
  • 長揖 - A formal bow, used to show respect to someone of higher status or to a deity.
  • 喪禍 - A calamity or misfortune, often used in the context of death or disaster.
  • 肉翼 - 'Fleshy wings,' a description of a supernatural or monstrous being, possibly a demon or spirit.
  • 青 - The color blue or green, often used in classical Chinese to describe supernatural beings or elements.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不孝于姑 - Classical expression meaning 'disobedient to her mother-in-law' or 'unfilial to her mother-in-law.' In Confucian ethics, filial piety to one’s mother-in-law is a significant virtue.
  • 每饭以麦 - She gave only barley for each meal. Implies a lack of respect or provision.
  • 不得饱 - Could not be satisfied or fed enough. Suggests that the food provided was insufficient.
  • 自食白秔饭 - She ate white glutinous rice herself. *秔* refers to glutinous rice, a more refined or valuable grain.
  • 从姑乞食 - He asked the mother-in-law for food. *乞食* is a humble term for begging for food.
  • 我自不曾饱 - I myself have never been full. A statement of personal deprivation.
  • 安得有余 - How can I have any excess? A rhetorical question.
  • 以此施我 - Give this to me. A direct request.
  • 白饭是七[女更]者 - This white rice is for the Seven [女更]. The term *女更* is likely a name or title, possibly a woman named 'Seven [女更]' or a term referring to a specific role.
  • 归来必遭骂辱 - If I return, I will be scolded and insulted. Implies fear of punishment.
  • 僧坚求不已 - The monk insisted persistently. Indicates refusal to give up.
  • 脱尔身上袈裟来 - Take off the robe on your body. A demand to remove the monk’s robe.
  • 戏披于身 - She playfully put it on. Indicates mockery or lack of seriousness.
  • 袈裟变为牛皮 - The robe turned into cowhide. A supernatural transformation.
  • 牢不可脱 - It was firm and could not be removed. Implies a physical or magical constraint.
  • 胸间先生毛一片 - A single hair began to grow on the chest. *生* here is used in the sense of 'to grow' or 'to appear.'
  • 渐遍四体 - Gradually spread over the body. Indicates a process of transformation.
  • 头面半成牛 - The head and face became half-cow. Describes the partial transformation.
  • 走报妇家 - Ran to inform the wife's family. *走* means 'to run' or 'to go quickly.'
  • 父母遽至 - Parents hurriedly arrived. *遽* means 'hurriedly' or 'immediately.'
  • 则俨然全牛矣 - Then it was clearly a full cow. *俨然* means 'clearly' or 'distinctly.'
  • 今不知存亡 - Now it is unknown whether it still exists. Indicates uncertainty about the current state.
  • 右四事亦得于王日严 - The above four stories were also obtained from Wang Rizheng. *右* here may mean 'the above' or 'the following.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 绍兴三十年 - The year 1160 in the Southern Song dynasty (1127–1279). The *Shaoxing* era was the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song.
  • 信州玉山县 - Xin Prefecture, Yushan County. *信州* (Xin Prefecture) is an ancient name for what is now Yushan County in Jiangxi Province.
  • 塘南七里店 - Seven Li Store to the south of the pond. A specific location within Yushan County.
  • 谢七妻 - The wife of Xie Seven. *谢七* is likely a common name or nickname.
  • 游僧 - Wandering monk. Refers to monks who travel from place to place, often associated with itinerant religious figures.
  • 乞食 - Begging for food. A common practice among monks, but here it implies a request from a layperson.
  • 袈裟 - Monk's robe. A symbol of monastic life and religious identity.
  • 女更 - This term is unusual. It may be a name, a title, or a term meaning 'female watchman' or 'female night guard.' However, given the context, it is more likely a name or title of a woman, possibly a nickname or honorific.
  • 牛皮 - Cowhide. Used here as a metaphor or magical transformation.
  • 四体 - Four limbs or body parts. Refers to the full human body in transformation.
  • 头面半成牛 - Head and face half-cow. Indicates a partial transformation, a common motif in Chinese folktales and supernatural stories.
  • 王日严 - Wang Rizheng. A likely compiler or collector of folktales or supernatural stories from the Song dynasty. The name is not widely known, so it may be a local or lesser-known figure.

Proper Nouns

  • 谢七妻 - The wife of Xie Seven.
  • 绍兴三十年 - The 30th year of the Shaoxing era (1160 CE).
  • 信州玉山县 - Xin Prefecture, Yushan County (modern-day Yushan County, Jiangxi Province).
  • 塘南七里店 - Seven Li Store to the south of the pond. A specific location within Yushan County.
  • 游僧 - Wandering monk. A general term for monks who travel.
  • 王日严 - Wang Rizheng. A likely compiler of the story, possibly a local scholar or collector of folktales from the Song dynasty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 干道 - The era name of Emperor Xiaozong of Song, a period marked by significant cultural and political developments in China.
  • 社日 - A traditional festival that reflects the importance of community and agricultural rituals in Chinese culture. It was a time for villagers to gather, offer sacrifices to the earth god, and celebrate communal bonds.
  • 土地神 - The land deity, also known as the 'Earth God' or 'Tudi Gong,' is a local deity worshipped in Chinese folk religion. The land deity is believed to protect the village and its inhabitants.
  • 先香火位 - Refers to the ancestral altar or the place where incense is burned to honor ancestors. This is a common feature in Chinese households, especially during important festivals or rituals.
  • 父祖列坐其所 - Describes the ancestral spirits sitting in the place where the man performed the ritual. This reflects the belief in ancestor worship and the continuity between the living and the dead in Chinese culture.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '跌于田坎水中' can be ambiguous. It could mean 'fell into a ditch or hole in the field' or 'fell into a water-filled ditch.' The exact nature of the fall is not clear from the text alone.
  • '急缘田塍行' describes the man running along the ridges of the fields. The term '缘' (to follow or trace) adds a sense of urgency to his movement.
  • '父曰。勿恐。吾为汝豤土地' is somewhat elliptical. A more complete version might be '父曰：勿恐，吾为汝祈土地' (The father said: Do not be afraid, I will pray for the land deity for you).
  • '即起。俄土地神至' is a compressed sequence of events. A more explicit version might be '即起，俄而土地神至' (He immediately stood up, and soon the land deity arrived).
  • '教民自抱其身' is somewhat ambiguous. It could mean 'told the man to hold himself' or 'instructed the man to embrace himself,' possibly as part of a ritual or to regain consciousness.
  • '大呼数声' means 'shouted several times,' but the exact nature of the shout is not specified. It could be a cry for help or a call to the land deity.

Proper Nouns

  • 干道 - The era name of Emperor Xiaozong of Song (1162–1189).
  • 张寅 - The name of the nephew who recounted the story. This is a proper noun that should be consistently translated as 'Zhang Yin.'

  • 林无差 - Nickname given to Lin Yi, meaning 'Lin without difference,' possibly indicating his fairness or lack of favoritism.
  • 贡士特奏名 - Refers to a special imperial examination system where scholars could be recommended for official positions without taking the regular exams.
  • 掾属数十辈 - Means 'dozens of subordinates or assistants,' indicating a large staff.
  • 金紫银章 - Refers to high-ranking officials' attire or insignia, with '金紫' (gold and purple) and '银章' (silver seals) symbolizing their status.
  • 印衔 - Refers to the seal or title of an official.
  • 二百四十年始一迁 - Indicates a long period of 240 years before a promotion, suggesting a low-ranking or stagnant position.
  • 冥司主掌 - Refers to the underworld or a spiritual realm where officials manage affairs.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 酆都 - A place in Chinese mythology associated with the afterlife, often depicted as a realm where souls are judged.
  • 贡士特奏名 - A historical imperial examination system in China, where scholars could be recommended for official positions without passing the regular exams.
  • 冥司 - The underworld or a spiritual realm in Chinese folklore, often associated with judgment and punishment.
  • 神仙事 - Refers to Daoist or Buddhist practices related to immortality or spiritual transcendence.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The title and name are combined in a way that is unclear whether it refers to a specific position or a general title.
  • This clause is ambiguous about whether the position is reserved for those who have been punished or whether it is a place where punishment occurs.
  • This clause is ambiguous about the consequences of refusing the position, whether it is a fear of divine retribution or a fear of being demoted.

Proper Nouns

  • 林乂 - The name of the protagonist, Lin Yi.
  • 酆都 - A place associated with the afterlife in Chinese mythology.
  • 冥司 - The underworld or a spiritual realm in Chinese folklore.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以告所善道士吕山友 - Told the good Daoist master Lu Shanyou. '所善' indicates a close or respected relationship.
  • 尚书策女也 - Daughter of Cè, a scholar-official. '尚书' refers to a high-ranking official, and '策女' means the daughter of such a person.
  • 不食猪肉 - Does not eat pork. Straightforward statement about dietary restrictions.
  • 吾家寒素 - Our family is poor and unremarkable. '寒素' is a humble self-description, indicating a lack of wealth or social standing.
  • 盍随家丰俭勉食之 - Why not eat according to the family's wealth or poverty? '盍' is an archaic interrogative particle, and '勉食之' means to try to eat.
  • 何敢尔 - How dare I? Humble expression of reluctance or fear.
  • 但新妇自少小时 - But since I was a young girl. '新妇' is a respectful term for a new wife.
  • 我若真为酆都官 - If I were truly an official of Fengdu. '酆都' refers to the underworld or a place of judgment in Chinese mythology.
  • 幸蒙伯力为增此食料 - Fortunately, I have been helped by your strength to add this food. '伯力' is a respectful term for a man's strength or power.
  • 新妇大愿也 - It is a great wish of the new wife. Expresses a strong desire or hope.
  • 卒于舟中 - Died in the boat. Describes the place of death.
  • 讣未至吴 - The news of his death had not yet reached Wu. '讣' refers to the announcement of someone's death.
  • 家人臛猪为面 - The family made a broth with pork to make a dish. '臛' refers to a broth or stew.
  • 取食之 - Took it and ate it. Describes the action of eating.
  • 立尽一器 - Finished a whole dish immediately. Emphasizes the speed and completeness of the action.
  • 自是遂能食 - From then on, she could eat it. Indicates a change in behavior or ability.
  • 时乂卒已半月云 - At that time, it had been half a month since Yì died. '云' is a particle indicating that the following statement is a report or hearsay.
  • 自山□□□宅编作记 - From the house of the mountain, the record was compiled. Ellipsis suggests missing or unclear text.
  • □一□不甚详 - One thing is not very detailed. Ellipsis indicates missing or unclear text.
  • 又□□以乂为毅 - Also, he was considered brave. Ellipsis suggests missing or unclear text.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 道士 - Refers to a Daoist priest or practitioner, indicating a religious or spiritual context.
  • 尚书 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the imperial bureaucracy, indicating a person of significant social status.
  • 酆都 - Refers to the underworld or a place of judgment in Chinese mythology, indicating a supernatural or spiritual realm.
  • 吴 - Refers to Wu, a historical region in China, indicating a specific geographical location.
  • 山友 - Refers to a Daoist master, indicating a religious or spiritual leader.
  • 策女 - Refers to the daughter of a scholar-official, indicating a specific social status.

Proper Nouns

  • 吕山友 - The name of a Daoist master.
  • 尚书策女 - The daughter of a scholar-official named Cè.
  • 酆都 - The underworld or a place of judgment in Chinese mythology.
  • 吴 - A historical region in China.
  • 乂 - The name of a person mentioned in the text.
  • 毅 - A term indicating bravery or courage.

  • 与世间所绘龙相类 - Means 'it was similar to the dragons depicted in the world.'
  • 忽大水犯都城 - Means 'suddenly, a great flood attacked the capital city.'
  • 高出十丈 - Means 'it rose ten zhang high.'
  • 民居尽没 - Means 'all the homes were submerged.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宣和元年 - The first year of the Xuanhe era, a reign title of Emperor Huizong of the Song Dynasty (1119).
  • 京师 - The capital city, which during the Song Dynasty was Kaifeng (also known as Dongjing or Eastern Capital).
  • 开封县前茶肆家 - A tea house located in front of the county office in Kaifeng.
  • 军器作坊 - A military armory workshop, indicating a state-run facility for producing weapons.
  • 街吏 - Local officials or city officials responsible for street administration.
  • 都人 - Means 'people of the capital' or 'the citizens of the capital.'
  • 龙 - The Chinese dragon, a mythical creature with cultural and symbolic significance.
  • 大河 - The Yellow River, which was a major river in China and often associated with flooding.
  • 汴渠 - The Bian River, a canal system in the north of China, crucial for transportation and irrigation.
  • 五丈河 - A river in the region, likely in Henan Province.
  • 梁山泺 - A lake or pond near Liangshan, which is famous in Chinese literature (e.g., Water Margin).
  • 蔡绦后史补 - A historical text by Cai Tao (蔡绦), a Song Dynasty scholar. Hou Shi Bu is a title meaning 'Supplement to Later History.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 夷堅丙志 - A collection of strange stories and anecdotes from the Song Dynasty, compiled by Zhang Yingyu. It is a significant work in Chinese literature that explores supernatural and mysterious events.
  • 龙虎山张天师 - Refers to the Celestial Master Zhang of Longhu Mountain, a high-ranking Taoist official. The Longhu Mountain is a famous Taoist center in Jiangxi Province, known for its spiritual significance and the lineage of Taoist masters.
  • 东岳行祠 - Refers to a shrine dedicated to the Eastern Peak, one of the Four Sacred Mountains of Chinese Taoism. The Eastern Peak is associated with the god of longevity and is a significant site for Taoist rituals and pilgrimages.
  • 黄衣卒 - Yellow-robed officials, often associated with the bureaucracy of the afterlife in Chinese folklore. They are typically depicted as enforcers or messengers of the supernatural realm.

  • 人世务礼法 - Means that human society is governed by rituals and laws.
  • 地府治人罪 - Indicates that the underworld punishes human sins.
  • 正自不同 - Means that they are fundamentally different.
  • 追在狱囚 - Refers to prisoners being pursued.
  • 列廷下约万人 - Describes approximately ten thousand prisoners lined up in the courtyard.
  • 皆荷铁校 - Means that they are all carrying iron collars, a symbol of punishment.
  • 传呼引第十人 - Indicates that the tenth person is called out.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 九天司命 - Refers to the Nine Heavens' Life-and-Death Register, a Daoist concept related to fate and destiny.
  • 上清宫 - Refers to the Palace of Purity, a Daoist palace associated with the highest deities.
  • 救苦天尊 - A title for Guanyin, the Bodhisattva of Compassion, who is often invoked for help in times of suffering.
  • 狼茫冠 - An uncertain term, possibly a type of crown or headdress, though its exact meaning is unclear.
  • 上天真宰 - Refers to a high-ranking deity or official from the heavens, often associated with divine justice and order.
  • 三界 - Refers to the three realms: heaven, earth, and hell, a fundamental concept in Chinese cosmology.
  • 地府 - Refers to the underworld, where souls are judged and punished.

Proper Nouns

  • 九天司命 - The Nine Heavens' Life-and-Death Register.
  • 上清宫 - The Palace of Purity.
  • 救苦天尊 - Guanyin, the Bodhisattva of Compassion.
  • 狼茫冠 - An uncertain term, possibly a type of crown or headdress.
  • 上天真宰 - A high-ranking deity or official from the heavens.
  • 三界 - The three realms: heaven, earth, and hell.
  • 地府 - The underworld.

[805]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 直符使乘云持牒下取 - A compound action phrase describing a supernatural being descending to retrieve someone; '直符使' is a celestial officer, '乘云' means 'riding the cloud,' '持牒' means 'holding a document,' and '下取' means 'coming down to fetch.'
  • 径至地 - Means 'directly to the ground,' indicating a direct descent.
  • 挟此人同上云去 - Means 'taking this person up to the cloud,' describing the act of lifting someone into the sky.
  • 锻治于前 - Means 'being forged in front of them,' describing the process of torture.
  • 楚毒备极 - Means 'the pain and suffering are at their utmost,' emphasizing the extreme nature of the torment.
  • 叔介问如何可救 - Means 'Shu Jie asks how to save,' introducing a question about salvation.
  • 除是转九天生神章一万遍 - Means 'except to recite the 'Nine Heavens Birth of the Divine Chapter' ten thousand times,' indicating a specific method of salvation.
  • 镬汤碚石矞律等狱 - Describes various types of prisons; '镬汤' means 'cauldron of boiling water,' '碚石' means 'hot stones,' '矞律' means 'sizzling iron,' and '狱' means 'prison.'
  • 叔介言敢问将军何姓 - Means 'Shu Jie says, 'I dare to ask what your name is,' introducing a question about the identity of the general.
  • 旧在人间姓王 - Means 'in the past, he was named Wang in the human world,' revealing the identity of the general.
  • 此问无姓 - Means 'this question has no name,' indicating that the general does not have a name in the underworld.
  • 每见世人设水陆请地府诸司 - Means 'every time I see people setting up water and land offerings to the officials of the underworld,' describing a common practice of making offerings.
  • 称崔判官李判官之类皆不肯赴 - Means 'calling officials like Cui Judge and Li Judge, they all refuse to come,' indicating that the officials do not respond to the offerings.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 直符使 - A celestial officer in Chinese mythology, often associated with the heavens and the afterlife.
  • 牒 - A document or scroll, often used in religious or legal contexts.
  • 九天生神章 - A religious text or scripture, often associated with salvation and spiritual practices.
  • 镬汤碚石矞律等狱 - Types of punishments in the underworld, reflecting traditional beliefs about the afterlife and karma.

Proper Nouns

  • 直符使 - A celestial officer.
  • 叔介 - The name of the person asking the question.
  • 九天生神章 - A religious text.

[806]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不若只称第几司第几案判官便了 - Colloquial expression meaning 'It would be better to just call the judge of the Xth bureau and Yth case.' The phrase '便了' suggests a practical, straightforward solution.
  • 吾得一幙次甚窄 - Literally means 'I got a very narrow tent,' but likely refers to a cramped or confined space, possibly a small office or workspace.
  • 身却不在彼 - Means 'my body is not there,' indicating physical absence from the place.
  • 常在坛上听指挥 - Means 'I am always on the altar listening to commands,' suggesting a role of being present and attentive in a ritual or ceremonial setting.
  • 不敢离一步 - Means 'I dare not leave a step,' indicating a strict adherence to one's duties or position.
  • 便一两字亦从吾手中过 - Means 'even a word or two must pass through my hands,' emphasizing meticulous attention to detail.
  • 然后奏上 - Means 'then I present it,' referring to the act of submitting a report or document.
  • 只为排得幙次不是 - Means 'because the tent (or office) was not arranged properly,' indicating a problem with organization or setup.
  • 左右多有秽触 - Means 'there were many impurities around,' suggesting a lack of cleanliness or propriety in the surroundings.
  • 黄衣人炷香衣服不洁 - Means 'the yellow-robed person's incense clothes were not clean,' indicating a failure to maintain proper ritual attire.
  • 负水人身体腥秽 - Means 'the water-carrying person's body was foul-smelling,' suggesting poor hygiene.
  • 一青衫小儿抱婴孩来天尊位前戏狎 - Means 'a green-shirted child brought a baby to play around the position of the Heavenly Venerable,' indicating a disruption of ritual decorum.
  • 青词甚好 - Means 'the green words (ritual texts) were very good,' praising the quality of the ritual writing.
  • 主者白请放六人 - Means 'the head official reported and requested the release of six people,' suggesting an appeal for leniency.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 三清 - Refers to the Three Pure Ones, the highest deities in Taoism, representing the ultimate reality.
  • 法师 - Refers to a Taoist priest or master, who conducts rituals and ceremonies.
  • 天尊 - Refers to a high-ranking deity, often the Heavenly Venerable or a Taoist god.
  • 青词 - Refers to ritual texts written on green paper, often used in Taoist ceremonies.
  • 地狱 - Refers to the underworld or hell in Chinese religious and mythological contexts.
  • 判官 - Refers to a judge or official in a ritual or mythological context, often associated with the underworld.
  • 黄衣人 - Refers to a person dressed in yellow, likely a Taoist priest or ritual attendant.
  • 负水人 - Refers to a person responsible for carrying water, possibly a ritual servant.
  • 青衫小儿 - Refers to a child dressed in green, likely a minor figure in a ritual or mythological setting.

Proper Nouns

  • 三清 - The Three Pure Ones, the highest deities in Taoism.
  • 法师 - A Taoist priest or master.
  • 天尊 - A high-ranking deity, often the Heavenly Venerable or a Taoist god.
  • 青词 - Ritual texts written on green paper.
  • 地狱 - The underworld or hell in Chinese religious and mythological contexts.
  • 判官 - A judge or official in a ritual or mythological context.
  • 黄衣人 - A person dressed in yellow, likely a Taoist priest or ritual attendant.
  • 负水人 - A person responsible for carrying water, possibly a ritual servant.
  • 青衫小儿 - A child dressed in green, likely a minor figure in a ritual or mythological setting.
